title: Supplementary Code Examples
text:
The main manuscript includes only short paired examples to illustrate selected design principles without overwhelming the paper. This page provides expanded versions of those examples.
These snippets are not intended to reproduce the entire applications. Full source code is available in the project repository.

The main manuscript includes only short paired examples to illustrate selected design principles without overwhelming the paper. This page provides expanded versions of those examples.

title: Supplementary Code Examples
section: Figure 2 — Separate data logic from display logic

title: Supplementary Code Examples
section: Figure 3 — Validate inputs at the boundary
text:
Figure 3 — Validate inputs at the boundary
This example illustrates the principle behind Rule 4: uploaded files should be checked immediately after import, before downstream filtering, plotting, or enrichment.

R Shiny
raw_data <- reactive({
  req(input$file)
  df <- read.csv(input$file$datapath)
  required <- c("gene", "logFC", "adj.P.Val")
  validate(
    need(
      all(required %in% names(df)),
      "Uploaded file is missing required columns."
    )
  )
  df
})


Python Shiny
@reactive.calc
def raw_data():
    f = input.file()
    if f is None:
        return None
    df = pd.read_csv(f[0]["datapath"])
    required = ["gene", "logFC", "adj.P.Val"]
    missing = [c for c in required if c not in df.columns]
    if missing:
        return None
    return df

title: Supplementary Code Examples
text:
Optional extended example — Downloadable reporting
The manuscript discusses downloadable reporting as part of Rule 6. Full implementation details are kept out of the paper body, but the applications support report generation that captures current analysis settings and outputs.
See the repository for the complete reporting logic used in the R and Python implementations.

title: Methods
text:
The manuscript is a Ten Simple Rules paper, not a software benchmarking study. The R Shiny and Python Shiny applications are provided as worked examples to illustrate transferable design principles for turning a bioinformatics analysis into an interactive web application.
The worked example used throughout the manuscript is a differential gene expression and Gene Ontology enrichment application that allows users to upload a results table, explore thresholds interactively, visualise outputs, and download a report capturing the current analysis state.

title: Methods
text:
The manuscript is a Ten Simple Rules paper, not a software benchmarking study. The R Shiny and Python Shiny applications are provided as worked examples to illustrate transferable design principles for turning a bioinformatics analysis into an interactive web application.
The worked example used throughout the manuscript is a differential gene expression and Gene Ontology enrichment application that allows users to upload a results table, explore thresholds interactively, visualise outputs, and download a report capturing the current analysis state.

title: Methods
text:
Validation dataset
The worked example uses GSE60450, a public mouse mammary gland RNA-seq dataset comparing luminal and basal cell populations across developmental stages.
This dataset was chosen because it is public, citable, and suitable for demonstrating a complete interactive workflow from differential expression results to downstream interpretation.

title: Methods
text:
Differential expression workflow

R implementation
The R implementation follows a Bioconductor-oriented workflow. Counts are normalised and analysed using a limma-based pipeline, and the resulting table is used as input for the interactive application.
This implementation serves as the reference implementation for the genomics workflow used in the manuscript.


Python implementation
The Python implementation is included to demonstrate the same application-design principles in a Python Shiny environment. It supports the same general interactive workflow, but some analytical steps differ because the surrounding Python ecosystem does not fully mirror the Bioconductor stack used in R.
For this reason, the Python implementation should be interpreted as an illustrative parallel implementation rather than as a claim of strict methodological equivalence.

title: Methods
text:
Gene Ontology enrichment

R implementation
The R implementation uses Bioconductor-oriented annotation and enrichment tooling and is suitable for environments where local annotation resources and offline operation are important.


Python implementation
The Python implementation uses Python-native enrichment tooling and external services where needed. This makes deployment straightforward in some settings, but can introduce internet dependence for parts of the workflow.

title: Methods
text:
Why both implementations are shown
The manuscript does not argue that one framework is universally superior. Instead, it uses both implementations to show that the same design principles can be applied across different ecosystems.
The practical choice between frameworks depends on the location of critical dependencies, the surrounding analytical environment, deployment constraints, and the needs of the intended users.

title: Methods
text:
Main practical differences
For the worked example in the manuscript, the most relevant differences are:

bioinformatics ecosystem maturity
offline versus internet-dependent enrichment
report-generation workflow
deployment and containerisation options

These trade-offs are summarised in Table 1 of the manuscript.

title: Methods
text:
Relationship to the manuscript figures
This website expands on material that was kept brief in the main manuscript.

Figure 1 presents the ten-rule workflow conceptually
Figure 2 illustrates separation of data logic from display logic
Figure 3 illustrates boundary validation of uploaded inputs

Expanded paired code examples corresponding to Figures 2 and 3 are provided on the Supplementary Code Examples page.

Recommended citation practice

title: Ten Simple Rules for Turning Bioinformatics Analyses into Interactive Web Applications
text:
This website supports the manuscript:

Ten Simple Rules for Turning Bioinformatics Analyses into Interactive Web Applications
Agudelo-Romero P, Caparros-Martin J, Sikazwe C, Blyth C.
[Journal and DOI].

The manuscript presents ten general rules for converting a bioinformatics analysis into a shareable interactive web application. This site provides the worked example, expanded implementation notes, supplementary code examples, and reproducibility material that were moved out of the main paper for brevity.

[Journal and DOI].

The manuscript presents ten general rules for converting a bioinformatics analysis into a shareable interactive web application. This site provides the worked example, expanded implementation notes, supplementary code examples, and reproducibility material that were moved out of the main paper for brevity.

title: Ten Simple Rules for Turning Bioinformatics Analyses into Interactive Web Applications
text:
Worked example
The worked example used throughout the manuscript is an interactive application for differential gene expression exploration and Gene Ontology enrichment. Two implementations are provided:

an R Shiny implementation, used as the reference implementation for the genomics workflow
a Python Shiny implementation, included as a parallel worked example illustrating the same design principles in a different ecosystem

Both applications accept a CSV derived from differential expression analysis and support interactive thresholding, visualisation, enrichment analysis, and downloadable reporting.
The validation dataset used in the worked example is GSE60450.

title: Reproducibility
text: This page provides practical information for restoring the environments, preparing the validation dataset, and running the worked-example applications locally.

title: Reproducibility
text: This page provides practical information for restoring the environments, preparing the validation dataset, and running the worked-example applications locally.

title: Reproducibility
text:
Repository contents
The repository contains:

the R Shiny implementation
the Python Shiny implementation
data-preparation scripts
dependency-lock files
manuscript companion website files

title: Reproducibility
text:
Restore the R environment
The R implementation uses a locked project environment.
Rscript -e "renv::restore()"

title: Reproducibility
text:
Restore the Python environment
The Python implementation uses an environment definition plus Python package requirements.
conda env create -f Python/shiny_app_py_env.yml
conda activate shiny_app_py
pip install -r Python/requirements.txt

title: Reproducibility
text:
Prepare the validation dataset
Use the included data-preparation scripts to obtain and format the public validation dataset used in the manuscript.

R workflow
Rscript R/00_setup.R
Rscript R/01_data_prep.R


Python workflow
python Python/00_setup.py
conda activate shiny_app_py
python Python/01_data_prep.py

title: Reproducibility
text:
Run the applications locally

R Shiny
Rscript -e "shiny::runApp('R')"


Python Shiny
shiny run Python/app.py

title: Reproducibility
text:
Deployment notes
The manuscript discusses deployment as a general principle rather than prescribing one hosting platform.
In the worked example:

the R Shiny implementation is suitable for standard Shiny-oriented deployment workflows
the Python Shiny implementation is suitable for Python-oriented deployment workflows, including container-based options

title: Reproducibility
text:
Troubleshooting notes

Uploaded file is rejected
Check that the uploaded file contains the expected column names and structure.


Python enrichment does not run
Some parts of the Python workflow may depend on internet-accessible services. Check server connectivity.


Environment restores but app still fails
Recreate the environment from the lock files rather than reusing a partially modified environment.


Results differ between R and Python
The two implementations are intended to illustrate the same application-design principles, not strict one-to-one analytical identity at every step of the workflow.
For the genomics example used in the manuscript, the R implementation should be treated as the reference implementation.
